_comment: Rudrāṣṭakam — eight verses in praise of Śiva, composed by Goswami Tulsidas (1532–1623) and embedded in the Uttarakāṇḍa of the Rāmcaritmānas (recited there by the sage Lomaśa). One of the most widely sung Śiva stotras; the meter is bhujaṅgaprayāta (4×ya — same meter as the Hari Stotram). Timestamps were supplied by the curator from the actual audio. Canonical Devanagari sourced from Rāmcaritmānas editions and Sanskrit Documents.
title: Rudrāṣṭakam (Tulsidas) — Namāmīśam-īśāna Nirvāṇa-rūpaṃ

lines.devanagari: नमामीशमीशान निर्वाणरूपं विभुं व्यापकं ब्रह्म-वेद-स्वरूपम् । निजं निर्गुणं निर्विकल्पं निरीहं चिदाकाशम् आकाश-वासं भजेऽहम् ॥१॥
lines.iast: namāmīśam īśāna nirvāṇa-rūpaṃ vibhuṃ vyāpakaṃ brahma-veda-svarūpam | nijaṃ nirguṇaṃ nirvikalpaṃ nirīhaṃ cid-ākāśam ākāśa-vāsaṃ bhajeham ||1||
lines.english_literal: I bow to the Lord, the ruler, of the form of nirvāṇa, the all-pervading, the omnipresent, whose true form is Brahman known by the Vedas. The very-own, attributeless, choiceless, desireless, the sky of consciousness, dwelling in space — I worship Him.
lines.english_poetic: I bow to Īśāna, whose form is liberation itself — all-pervading, omnipresent, the very being whom the Vedas call Brahman. Native and bare of qualities, free of all alternative, beyond all wanting — the open sky of consciousness whose home is space itself — I worship Him.
lines.explanation: Verse 1 of the Rudrāṣṭakam — the opening salutation to Śiva as the absolute. Note the cascade of via-negativa adjectives in pāda 3: nirguṇa (without attributes), nirvikalpa (without alternative — non-dual), nirīha (without desire). 'Cid-ākāśa' (sky of consciousness) is a hallmark Tantric phrase: pure awareness pictured as boundless space.

words.meaning: whose true form is the Brahman of the Vedas

lines.devanagari: निराकारम् ओङ्कारमूलं तुरीयं गिराज्ञान-गोतीतम् ईशं गिरीशम् । करालं महाकाल-कालं कृपालं गुणागार संसार-पारं नतोऽहम् ॥२॥
lines.iast: nirākāram oṃkāra-mūlaṃ turīyaṃ girā-jñāna-gotītam īśaṃ girīśam | karālaṃ mahākāla-kālaṃ kṛpālaṃ guṇāgāra saṃsāra-pāraṃ nato'ham ||2||
lines.english_literal: Formless, root of OM, the fourth (turīya), beyond speech-knowledge-cognition, the Lord, Lord of the mountain. Terrible, death of even great-Death, compassionate, abode of qualities, beyond saṃsāra — I bow.
lines.english_poetic: Formless, the root from which OM springs, the fourth state beyond word and knowledge and grasp, the Lord — the Lord of mountains. Awesome, the death of even great Death, full of compassion, the treasury of every quality, the far shore beyond saṃsāra — to You I bow.
lines.explanation: Verse 2 develops the paradox of Śiva — simultaneously formless (nirākāra) yet identified with form (the mountain-dweller of Kailāsa, girīśa). 'Mahākāla-kāla' literally means 'the time of even Great-Time' — Śiva is the destroyer even of Death itself. 'Turīya' is the fourth state of consciousness from the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — pure awareness beyond waking, dream, deep sleep.

words.meaning: beyond the senses (go = sense organ + atīta = beyond)
Lord of the mountain (Kailāsa); epithet of Śiva

lines.devanagari: तुषाराद्रि-संकाश-गौरं गभीरं मनोभूत-कोटि-प्रभा श्री शरीरम् । स्फुरन्-मौलि-कल्लोलिनी चारु-गङ्गा लसद्-भाल-बालेन्दु कण्ठे भुजङ्गा ॥३॥
lines.iast: tuṣārādri-saṃkāśa-gauraṃ gabhīraṃ manobhūta-koṭi-prabhā śrī śarīram | sphuran-mauli-kallolinī cāru-gaṅgā lasad-bhāla-bālendu kaṇṭhe bhujaṅgā ||3||
lines.english_literal: Fair like a snow-mountain, deep — body with the splendour of a crore of Manmathas (love-gods), of glorious form. The lovely Gaṅgā as a wave-bearing river quivers on the head, the crescent moon (bāla-indu) shines on the brow, on the throat the serpent.
lines.english_poetic: Fair as the snow-clad mountain, deep and unmoved — His glorious body radiant with the splendour of a crore Cupids. The Gaṅgā rolls in waves over His matted hair, the young crescent moon glows on His brow, and around His throat — the cobra.
lines.explanation: Verse 3 turns from the metaphysical Śiva to the iconographic Śiva: the snow-white body, the Gaṅgā cascading from his matted locks, the crescent moon on his forehead, the serpent around his throat. 'Manobhūta-koṭi-prabhā' compares his radiance to ten million Kāmadevas (the god of love, also called Manobhū / Manmatha) — a Sanskrit hyperbole for unmatched beauty.

lines.devanagari: चलत्-कुण्डलं भ्रू-सुनेत्रं विशालं प्रसन्नाननं नीलकण्ठं दयालम् । मृगाधीश-चर्माम्बरं मुण्डमालं प्रियं शंकरं सर्वनाथं भजामि ॥४॥
lines.iast: calat-kuṇḍalaṃ bhrū-sunetraṃ viśālaṃ prasannānanaṃ nīlakaṇṭhaṃ dayālam | mṛgādhīśa-carmāmbaraṃ muṇḍamālaṃ priyaṃ śaṃkaraṃ sarvanāthaṃ bhajāmi ||4||
lines.english_literal: With swinging earrings, with brows and beautiful eyes, broad-shouldered, with serene face, blue-throated, compassionate. Wearing the lion's skin as a garment, with a garland of skulls — the dear Śaṃkara, Lord of all — I worship.
lines.english_poetic: Ear-rings swaying, brows over wide and beautiful eyes, broad-framed, with a serene face — blue-throated, full of compassion. Clad in a lion's skin, garlanded with skulls — Śaṃkara the beloved, Lord of all — Him I worship.
lines.explanation: Verse 4 continues the iconography. 'Nīlakaṇṭha' (blue-throat) recalls the legend of the Sāgara-manthana: Śiva swallowed the deadly halāhala poison to save the universe, and held it in his throat — turning it forever blue. 'Mṛgādhīśa-carmāmbara' (lion-skin garment) and 'muṇḍa-māla' (skull-garland) are the renunciate-Śiva of the cremation grounds.

lines.devanagari: प्रचण्डं प्रकृष्टं प्रगल्भं परेशं अखण्डम् अजं भानु-कोटि-प्रकाशम् । त्रयः-शूल-निर्मूलनं शूल-पाणिं भजेऽहं भवानी-पतिं भाव-गम्यम् ॥५॥
lines.iast: pracaṇḍaṃ prakṛṣṭaṃ pragalbhaṃ pareśaṃ akhaṇḍam ajaṃ bhānu-koṭi-prakāśam | trayaḥ-śūla-nirmūlanaṃ śūla-pāṇiṃ bhajeham bhavānī-patiṃ bhāva-gamyam ||5||
lines.english_literal: Fierce, excellent, bold, the supreme Lord, undivided, unborn, of the splendour of a crore suns. Uprooter of the threefold spike (of suffering), trident-handed — I worship the husband of Bhavānī, attainable by feeling.
lines.english_poetic: Fierce and pre-eminent, bold and resolute, the supreme Lord — undivided, unborn, radiant as a crore suns. He who tears out by the root the three-pronged spike of suffering — the Trident-bearer — I worship Him, the consort of Bhavānī, who is found by the heart that feels.
lines.explanation: Verse 5 — Śiva as cosmic warrior. 'Trayaḥ-śūla-nirmūlana' has two readings: literally 'uprooter of the trident' (referring to his own weapon), or 'uprooter of the threefold spike' — the three sufferings of saṃsāra (ādhyātmika, ādhibhautika, ādhidaivika — those born of self, environment, and divine forces). 'Bhāva-gamya' — attainable only by bhāva (devotional feeling), not by intellect alone.

words.meaning: with the splendour of a crore suns

lines.devanagari: कलातीत-कल्याण-कल्पान्तकारी सदा सज्जनानन्द-दाता पुरारी । चिदानन्द-संदोह मोहापहारी प्रसीद प्रसीद प्रभो मन्मथारी ॥६॥
lines.iast: kalātīta-kalyāṇa-kalpāntakārī sadā sajjanānanda-dātā purārī | cidānanda-saṃdoha mohāpahārī prasīda prasīda prabho manmathārī ||6||
lines.english_literal: Beyond all parts, auspicious, the destroyer at the end of the kalpa. Always the giver of bliss to the good, destroyer of the Three Cities. Mass of consciousness-bliss, remover of delusion — be gracious, be gracious, O Lord, destroyer of Manmatha!
lines.english_poetic: Beyond all measure, auspicious — He who brings on the dissolution at the end of the kalpa. Always the giver of bliss to the good, the destroyer of the Three Cities (Tripura). Mass of consciousness-bliss, remover of delusion — be gracious, be gracious, O Lord, destroyer of the love-god!
lines.explanation: Verse 6 — direct petition. 'Purārī' (enemy of the cities) refers to Śiva's mythic destruction of Tripura, the three sky-cities built by the asura sons of Tāraka. 'Manmathārī' (enemy of Manmatha) recalls his burning of Kāmadeva when the love-god tried to break his meditation. 'Cidānanda-saṃdoha' — a 'collection / mass' of pure consciousness-bliss — is a high Vedantic phrase. The doubled 'prasīda prasīda' is the emotional fulcrum of the whole hymn.

words.meaning: doer of the end of the kalpa (cosmic dissolver)
giver of bliss to the good

lines.devanagari: न यावद् उमानाथ-पादारविन्दं भजन्तीह लोके परे वा नराणाम् । न तावत् सुखं शान्ति सन्ताप-नाशं प्रसीद प्रभो सर्व-भूताधिवासम् ॥७॥
lines.iast: na yāvad umānātha-pādāravindaṃ bhajantīha loke pare vā narāṇām | na tāvat sukhaṃ śānti santāpa-nāśaṃ prasīda prabho sarva-bhūtādhivāsam ||7||
lines.english_literal: Until people worship the lotus-feet of the Lord of Umā, in this world or in the next, until then there is no happiness, no peace, no removal of affliction. Be gracious, O Lord, the indweller of all beings.
lines.english_poetic: Until people bow at the lotus-feet of the Lord of Umā — in this world or any beyond — until then there is no happiness, no peace, no end to inner burning. Be gracious, O Lord, You who dwell within every being.
lines.explanation: Verse 7 — the theological core of the hymn. The 'yāvad… tāvad…' (until… until then…) structure makes a strict conditional: peace is not available outside devotion to Śiva. 'Sarva-bhūtādhivāsa' (indweller of all beings) anticipates the deeply Vedantic close: the divine isn't elsewhere, it's the antaryāmin.

words.meaning: until not, as long as not

lines.devanagari: न जानामि योगं जपं नैव पूजां नतोऽहं सदा सर्वदा शम्भु तुभ्यम् । जरा-जन्म-दुःखौघ-तातप्यमानं प्रभो पाहि आपन्नमामीश शम्भो ॥८॥
lines.iast: na jānāmi yogaṃ japaṃ naiva pūjāṃ nato'haṃ sadā sarvadā śambhu tubhyam | jarā-janma-duḥkhaugha-tātapyamānaṃ prabho pāhi āpannam īśa śambho ||8||
lines.english_literal: I do not know yoga, nor japa, nor worship. I am always bowing, in every way, to You, O Śambhu. Tormented by the flood of sorrows of age and birth, O Lord, protect the afflicted — O Lord, O Śambhu!
lines.english_poetic: I know no yoga, no japa, not even how to make pūjā — I only bow, always and in every way, to You, O Śambhu. Burnt by the flood of grief that is age and birth and dying — protect this stricken one, O Lord, O Śambhu!
lines.explanation: Verse 8 — the final cry. The hymn closes not with metaphysical claim but with humble confession: the devotee knows none of the sophisticated paths — neither yoga, nor mantra-repetition, nor ritual — and asks for grace anyway. 'Āpanna' (one who has fallen into trouble) is the petitioner's posture. This personal humility after seven verses of cosmic praise is what gives the Rudrāṣṭakam its enduring devotional power.
